EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front crossed the Ohio Valley on Monday, December 15th. On Tuesday the 16th, an upper level low pressure system tracked to the northeast, bringing a quick shot of snow to northern Kentucky and south central Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A half inch of snow was measured in Anderson Park, with 1.3 inches falling in Barnesburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bitter cold snap claimed the life of a Greene county woman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman was found outside of her home late Sunday night. She died after being taken to an area hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced widespread wind damage and power outages from downed wires across southeast Ohio and northern West Virginia.

Low pressure tracked through the central Great Lakes on the 8th, and continued to slowly track into Ontario on the 9th. Southerly winds ahead of this low pressure allowed for many locations in northern Ohio to report record high temperatures in the 60s during the day on the 8th. The low pressure intensified rapidly as it moved over the Great Lakes during this time. A strong cold front extended from this low pressure, and tracked across the area during the mornning hours of the 9th. A line of showers and thunderstorms developed just ahead of the cold front causing some thunderstorm wind gust damage. Behind the cold front, the strong low pressure allowed for windy conditions to continue through the morning hours of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees, large limbs, and power poles were reported down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure track north along a cold front from the lower Mississippi River Valley during the day on the 29th and through the central Great Lakes region on the 30th. This area of low pressure intensified as it tracked north through the central Great Lakes from the afternoon on the 29th through the 30th as this low pressure moved into Canada. The cold front associated with this low pressure moved through the region during the early overnight hours into the 30th. Severe thunderstorms developed ahead of the cold front during the late evening on the 29th, but windy conditions continued through the overnight hours as the low pressure intensifed. Winds finally subsided during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down across the county, especially in Massillon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front dropped south through the area during the day on the 19th. Northerly winds behind this front flowing over Lake Erie allowed for lake effect snow to develop through the daytime hours of the 19th. The lake effect snow continued into the 20th and tapered off on the 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow began during the morning hours of the 19th and continued through the day. The lake effect snow persisted through the overnight and into the daytime hours of the 20th before coming to an end that night. The snow was light during the daytime of the 19th, with locations picking up between 1.0 and 4.0 inches by the evening. Lake effect snow was heaviest during the overnight hours into the 20th. On the morning of the 20th in just 12 hours Concord measured 8.1 inches and Painesville measured 6.3 inches. The snow continued that day and came to an end during the late evening and early overnight hours. By the evening, many locations throughout the county measured between 6.0 and 10.0 inches of snow. By the end of the event, Madison measured 11.0 inches, two spotters in Concord measured 11.9 inches and 15 inches, 8.2 inches in Mentor, and 7.2 inches in Kirtland. Many accidents were reported from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front dropped south through the area during the day on the 19th. Northerly winds behind this front flowing over Lake Erie allowed for lake effect snow to develop through the daytime hours of the 19th. The lake effect snow continued into the 20th and tapered off on the 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the morning hours of the 19th and continued through the day. The snow was rather light during the day and by evening many locations only measured between 1.0 and 3.0 inches. The lake effect snow persisted through the overnight hours into the 20th, and was moderate to heavy at times overnight. By the morning of the 20th, snow reports just from the overnight hours indicate that Chardon picked up 9.0 inches, Hambden Township measured 7.6 inches and Montville measured 6.5 inches. The snow continued through much of the day on the 20th and ended that evening. Total snowfall measurements from this event include Chardon with 13.0 inches, Hambden with 12.3 inches, Montville measured 10.0 inches, and Thompson had 13.9 inches. Many accidents were reported from this event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms tracked through the area during the early morning hours of the 9th. A public call was received describing significant damage in Berlin Heights about trampolines wrapped around trees, homes losing shingles, and pine trees snapped. A survey was conducted that morning, and it was found that severe winds blew apart a roof of a porch that was attached to the house. This portion of the roof consequently tore apart much of the roof structure on the front side of the home, and as the roof blew over the top of the house a chimney and attenna were also destroyed. During the survey, there was confirmation on other pine trees that were down, along with the trampolines destroyed.

An area of low pressure passed to the north of Lake Erie during the morning hours of November 12th.  A cold front trailing the low swept east across Northeast Ohio by midday.  Behind the front, cold northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the morning of the 20th.  This activity intensified during the evening of the 20th and early morning hours of the 21st.  The snow showers eventually tapered off early on the 22nd after dropping significant accumulations on portions of inland Northeast Ohio.  Storm totals of over 20 inches of snow were reported at a few locations.  Travel was severely hampered and many accidents reported during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers began during the mornings hours of November 20th and intensified during the evening hours.  This activity continued through the 21st and finally tapered off early on the 22nd.  Six to eight inches of snow was reported along the Ashtabula County lake shore by daybreak on the 21st.  A peak total of 9.8 inches was measured in Conneaut with most of this falling before mid morning on the 21st.  Gusty northwest winds accompanied the snow showers and caused a lot of blowing and drifting.  Travel was severely hampered during this event and many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over Michigan on the afternoon of the 2nd ahead of a cold front.  These storms consolidated into a bow echo which moved rapidly eastward through southern Michigan and into Lake Erie near sunset.  The southern flank of this bow echo stalled across northwestern Ohio, with several rounds of thunderstorm redevelopment bringing heavy rainfall, flash flooding, and isolated severe weather to the Toledo area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was observed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rainfall started across Lucas County around 7:30 pm, with the heaviest rain falling between 8 pm and 9 pm, and then again between 12:30 am and 1:30 am.  Rainfall totals in the area included 5.00 inches at the intersection of I-475 and US 23, 4.31 inches in Sylvania, 3.70 inches at the Toledo airport, and 3.39 inches in Point Place.  Widespread flooding resulted, with many cars stranded citywide in underpasses and along flooded streets.  Flooding occurred in neighborhoods along several area drainages, including Shantee Creek, Swan Creek, and Heldman Ditch.  Over 100 basements were flooded from the excess runoff, and the Oregon municipal building was completely surrounded by water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through the central Great Lakes on the 8th, and continued to slowly track into Ontario on the 9th. Southerly winds ahead of this low pressure allowed for many locations in northern Ohio to report record high temperatures in the 60s during the day on the 8th. The low pressure intensified rapidly as it moved over the Great Lakes during this time. A strong cold front extended from this low pressure, and tracked across the area during the mornning hours of the 9th. A line of showers and thunderstorms developed just ahead of the cold front causing some thunderstorm wind gust damage. Behind the cold front, the strong low pressure allowed for windy conditions to continue through the morning hours of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down across a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure tracked from the southern Appalachians to western Virginia by Saturday March 8th. A deepening upper level trough worked into the Ohio Valley overnight, combining with the surface low and strengthening the entire system. The surface pressure gradient tightened in response to the upper low, and gusts of 30 to 40 mph brought blizzard-like conditions to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow was measured in Celina. Up to a foot of snow was estimated in the southern part of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small streams, such as Jonathan Creek, flooded and blocked roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure was located over southern Ontario on the morning of the 22nd, and an upper trough of low pressure was swinging southeast towards the region. This trough of low pressure, combined with a humid and unstable airmass across the area, caused showers and thunderstorms to develop beginning around the midday hours. Many of the thunderstorms across the area became severe through the afternoon, and tapered off during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was observed.

This trough of low pressure, combined with a humid and unstable airmass across the area, caused showers and thunderstorms to develop beginning around the midday hours. Many of the thunderstorms across the area became severe through the afternoon, and tapered off during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure was located near Lake Superior. An upper trough of low pressure was approaching the region during the late morning hours. The upper trough of low pressure combined with warm and humid air at the surface to allow for showers and thunderstorms to develop by midday across northwestern Ohio. These showers and thunderstorms continued east through the afternoon hours. Many of the thunderstorms became severe across the area. By late in the afternoon, the severe thunderstorms progressed through northeast Ohio and into northwest Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large limbs and power lines were reported down. Also, a few windows were broken at a hotel.

This trough of low pressure, combined with a humid and unstable airmass across the area, caused showers and thunderstorms to develop beginning around the midday hours. Many of the thunderstorms across the area became severe through the afternoon, and tapered off during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging from penny to quarter size was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another area of low pressure tracked across central Indiana into central Ohio. Snow was spread north of the low but remained confined to mainly Putnam, Allen and Van Wert counties where 4 to 6 inches of snow fell along with blowing and drifting snow, causing difficult travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported five to six inches of snow across the county, along with blowing and drifting of the new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rains of around a half inch fell in less than 3 hours just before dawn on Friday, the 4th.  This was out ahead of an approaching cold front.  Prior to these heavier showers, widespread rain fell on Thursday, the 3rd, from a warm front.  Total rainfall in less than 36 hours was 1.5 to 2.25 inches across Lawrence and Gallia Counties.||Minor small stream flooding occurred on Friday morning.  A few of the specific streams included Indian Guyan Creek in Lawrence County.  It flooded Route 218 near Platform.  In Greenfield Township of Gallia County, Dirtyface Creek flooded.||No structural damage to dwelling was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system producing very heavy rainfall was anchored over southeast Franklin county and northern Fairfield county early in the morning of the 26th. Several creeks and streams were out of their banks later in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were flooded and a few creeks had risen out of their banks across the southeast part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure passed to the north of Lake Erie during the morning hours of November 12th.  A cold front trailing the low swept east across Northeast Ohio by midday.  Behind the front, cold northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the morning of the 20th.  This activity intensified during the evening of the 20th and early morning hours of the 21st.  The snow showers eventually tapered off early on the 22nd after dropping significant accumulations on portions of inland Northeast Ohio.  Storm totals of over 20 inches of snow were reported at a few locations.  Travel was severely hampered and many accidents reported during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers began during the mornings hours of November 20th and intensified during the evening hours.  This activity continued through the 21st and finally tapered off early on the 22nd.  Six to twelve inches of snow was reported over portions of Ashtabula County by daybreak on the 21st.  The greatest snowfall amounts were reported over the east central portion of the county during this event.  A peak total of 24.7 inches was measured in Pierpont with most of this falling before mid morning on the 21st.  Gusty northwest winds accompanied the snow showers and caused a lot of blowing and drifting.  Travel was severely hampered during this event and many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong low pressure passed to the north of Lake Erie during the evening hours of November 24th and the early morning hours of November 25th.  A cold front trailing the low exited Northeast Ohio around midnight on the 25th.  Behind the front, cold northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the morning of the 25th.  This activity intensified during the afternoon of the 25th and tapered off on the morning of the 26th.  Up to nine inches of snow fell in northern Geauga County during this event.  A few accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers developed during the morning hours of November 25th behind a strong cold front.  This activity intensified during the afternoon of the 25th and finally tapered off on the morning of the 26th.  Locally heavy snowfall was reported in Geauga County.  Many locations saw between 6 and 8 inches of snow, most of it falling during the late afternoon and early evening hours of the 25th.  A peak total of 9 inches was reported at Chardon.  A few accidents occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain began as freezing rain late in the afternoon on the 23rd. Air temperatures were in the upper 20s and lower 30s.  The ground was cold due to the temperatures in the single digits and teens on the 22nd.||A thin ice coating, well under a quarter of an inch, formed for the late afternoon commute home.||The sheriff's office reported numerous minor vehicular accidents due to the icy roads. ||The temperature rose above freezing during the evening.

An area of low pressure passed well to the north of Lake Erie late on November 9th and early on the 10th.  A cold front trailing this low moved across the area during evening hours of the 9th.  Rain showers changed to snow showers late in the evening as colder air filtered into the area behind the front.  The snow showers tapered off during the afternoon hours of the 10th after several inches of accumulation.  Most of this accumulation occurred on the higher terrain just inland from Lake Erie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect rain showers changed to snow showers during the evening hours of November 9th.  These snow showers intensified during the early morning hours of November 10th and finally tapered off during the afternoon.  Snow accumulations of over six inches were reported on the higher terrain just inland from Lake Erie.  A maximum of 6.5 inches was measured in Concord Township.  A few accidents were reported.

The snow showers tapered off during the afternoon hours of the 10th after several inches of accumulation.  Most of this accumulation occurred on the higher terrain just inland from Lake Erie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect rain showers changed to snow showers during the evening hours of November 9th.  These snow showers intensified during the early morning hours of November 10th and finally tapered off during the afternoon.  Snow accumulations of six to eight inches were reported just inland from Lake Erie.  A maximum of 8.2 inches was measured in the City of Ashtabula.  A few accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong low pressure passed to the north of Lake Erie on November 15th.  Persistent northwest flow behind this system ushered much colder air into the region.  Temperatures became cold enough for lake effect snow showers to develop during the early afternoon hours of November 16th.  This activity intensified during the evening hours of the 16th and early morning hours of the 17th.  The snow showers finally tapered off during the morning hours of the 18th.  Significant snow accumulation occurred on the higher terrain inland from Lake Erie.  Gusty northwest winds accompanied the snow showers and caused some blowing and drifting.  Many accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers began during the early afternoon hours of November 16th.  These snow showers intensified during the evening hours of the 16th.  The snow showers gradually lessened on the 17th but didn't end completely till the morning hours of the 18th.  Gusty northwest winds accompanied the snow showers and caused some blowing and drifting.  Three day total snow accumulations exceeded a foot over much of northern Geauga County.  Most of this accumulation occurred late on the 16th and early on the 17th.  A maximum of 16.4 inches was measured by a spotter near Thompson. Gusty northwest winds accompanied the snow showers and caused some blowing and drifting. Many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low moved south across the eastern Great Lakes and combined with daytime heating produced thunderstorms across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia. Isolated flash flooding and severe thunderstorms occurred across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported nickel size hail in Homeworth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front moving across the Ohio Valley. A severe thunderstorm occurred over Belmont county in eastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported one inch hail near Bethesda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front moving across the Ohio Valley. A severe thunderstorm occurred over Belmont county in eastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down in Barnesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure passed to the north of Lake Erie during the morning hours of November 12th.  A cold front trailing the low swept east across Northeast Ohio by midday.  Behind the front, cold northwest winds blowing across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the morning of the 20th.  This activity intensified during the evening of the 20th and early morning hours of the 21st.  The snow showers eventually tapered off early on the 22nd after dropping significant accumulations on portions of inland Northeast Ohio.  Storm totals of over 20 inches of snow were reported at a few locations.  Travel was severely hampered and many accidents reported during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers began during the mornings hours of November 20th and intensified during the evening hours.  This activity continued through the 21st and finally tapered off early on the 22nd.  Six to eight inches of snow was reported over much of northern Geauga County by daybreak on the 21st.  Total accumulations for the event ranged from 8 to 16 inches across northern Geauga County with lesser amounts further south.  A peak total of 19.0 inches was measured in Bainbridge.  Gusty northwest winds accompanied the snow showers and caused a lot of blowing and drifting.  Travel was severely hampered during this event and many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved northeast into southwest Ohio during the overnight hours of the 1st, then over Lake Erie during the morning hours. Warm air moved over the area intially causing a mix of wintry  precipitation in some locations. However, this wintry mix of precipitation was short lived. Colder air moved in quickly changing the precipitation back to snow during the morning hours of the 1st. The snow eventually tapered off on the morning of the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the morning hours of the 1st and continued through the day. The precipitation began with a wintry mix of freezing rain, snow and sleet, but quickly changed over to all snow by mid morning. During the afternoon and evening hours snow was moderate to heavy with visibilities dropping to around a quarter mile at times. By the evening snow spotters reported 4.0 inches in Champion and Mesopotamia. Reports during the late evening hours from highway departments indicate that between 6 and 10 inches of snow fell during the day on the 1st. The snow continued overnight, and by the morning of the 2nd snowfall totals ranged between 6 and 12 inches. Many accidents were reported during the day on the 1st through the overnight hours into the 2nd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began across the area during the morning hours of the 1st, and continued through the entire day. The snow was moderate to heavy at times across the area during the afternoon hours. For a little more than an hour during the afternoon heavy snow was reported at Ashtabula County Airport with visibilities at one quarter of a mile. By evening, snow spotter already reported 10.5 inches in Pierpont, 8.0 inches in Windsor, and 4.0 inches in Dorsett. The snow continued into the overnight hours and tapered off during the early morning of the 2nd. Snowfall reports that morning brought the total snowfall in Pierpont to 14.0 inches, 8.3 in Windsor, and 7.0 inches in Dorsett. Many accidents were reported from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm air overspread the Ohio Valley ahead of a cold front during the late evening hours of the 23rd. Shallow cold air in place over west central Ohio permitted this rain to cause significant icing. Surface temperatures lagged somewhat, delaying the melting of the ice until later overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quarter inch of ice was reported in Fort Recovery.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous accidents due to significant icing were reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain blanketed portions of northwest Ohio with a thin sheet of ice during the morning hours of December 26th. Precipitation developed ahead of an approaching warm front, with temperatures generally in the mid to upper 20s as the freezing rain developed. Surface temperatures warmed above freezing by late in the morning, which changed the precipitation over to rain and drizzle. Numerous accidents and slide-offs were reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of freezing rain and freezing drizzle led to icy roadways during the morning hours of December 26th. The icy roadways led to numerous accidents and slide-offs with one fatality. A 23 year old woman was killed in a car accident when she lost control of her vehicle and slid into the path of another vehicle. This fatal accident occurred just east of Tedrow. Ice accumulations were generally less than 0.20 across the county.

Colder air moved in quickly changing the precipitation back to snow during the morning hours of the 1st. The snow eventually tapered off on the morning of the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began across the area during the early morning hours of the 1st and continued through the day. During the afternoon hours, the snow was moderate to heavy at times. By evening, as much as 12.0 inches was measured by a snow spotter in Newbury, and 7.8 inches in Chardon. The snow continued overnight and tapered off around daybreak on the 2nd. Twenty four hour totals include 14.2 inches in Chagrin Falls, 9.1 inches in Thompson, 12 inches in Chardon, and 12.1 inches in Claridon Township. Many accidents were reported across the area during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked through central Ohio on the 19th. The low pressure remained just south of the region, but warmer air aloft moved over the area allowing for mainly freezing rain across the region. There was some snow accumulation during this event, but most locations saw at least a quarter inch of freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation began during the overnight hours of the 19th as a mix of snow and sleet. Only a few tenths of snow was reported across the area before precipitation changed over to freezing rain. Freezing rain continued for several hours around daybreak, and around a quarter inch of ice was reported. However, as temperatures warmed up the freezing rain changed to rain. Impacts across the area were minimal with temperatures just a few degrees below freezing before sunrise, then warmed above freezing through the morning. A few accidents were reported across the area.

Impacts across the area were minimal with temperatures only a few degrees below freezing initially, then rising above freezing through the morning. Some accidents were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure passed to the north of Lake Erie during the morning hours of December 21st.  A strong cold front trailing this low swept east across Northeast Ohio.  Cold air moving across Lake Erie behind this front caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the afternoon of the 21st.  Heavy bands of lake effect snow affected the immediate lake shore area.  The heaviest snow fell during the evening hours of the 21st and the afternoon hours of the 22nd.  Strong winds accompanied the snow showers and caused considerable blowing and drifting. Storm total accumulations exceeded a foot in at a couple locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers developed during the afternoon of December 21st and continued into the evening hours of the 22nd.  Bands of snow affected the immediate lake shore area with the heaviest snow reported during the evening hours of the 21st and the afternoon of the 22nd.  Southwest to west winds gusting to as much as 35 mph accompanied the snow and caused near whiteout conditions at times.  Significant drifting was also reported.  This activity finally diminished during the evening of the 22nd as winds became more southwesterly.  Six to nine inches of snow was reported over portions of Lake County during this event.  A maximum of 10.0 inches was reported at Madison.  Travel was severely hampered during this event and many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved east across northern Ohio during the afternoon and early evening hours of December 24th.  Gusty winds occurred behind this front.  Peak winds at most locations were between 45 and 50 mph.  However, a few higher gusts were recorded by automated wind sensors.  A few trees and power lines were reported down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An automated wind sensor at the Findlay Airport measured a 58 mph wind gust at 5:00 pm.  A few trees and large limbs were downed.  A few small power outages were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked through central Ohio on the 19th. The low pressure remained just south of the region, but warmer air aloft moved over the area allowing for mainly freezing rain across the region. There was some snow accumulation during this event, but most locations saw at least a quarter inch of freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation began as a mix of snow and sleet during the overnight hours into the 19th, then quickly changed over to freezing rain. Freezing rain continued for several hours prior to daybreak. A quarter inch of ice was reported just after daybreak. However, with temperatures right around freezing impacts from the ice were minimal. In fact, shortly after sunrise temperatures rose above freezing allowing for a changeover to rain, and any ice quickly melted. A few accidents were reported, but no major power outages in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation began during the overnight hours of the 19th as a mixture of sleet and freezing rain. While around a half an inch of freezing rain was measured by automated weather sensing equipment at Mansfield Lahm Airport, the impacts were rather minimal. Temperatures were several degrees below freezing during the first few hours of the precipitation, but warmed up to around freezing by daybreak. As the morning progressed, temperatures warmed above freezing causing ice in the area to quickly melt. A few accidents were reported across the area, but no serious power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked through the northern Great Lakes during the overnight and morning hours of the 28th. A cold front moved through the region. While there were mainly just showers associated with the cold front, these showers were heavy enough to bring down severe winds. Once the cold front passed, synoptic winds associated with the strong low pressure system allowed for high winds to continue in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large limbs were reported down. Power outages were also reported in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers, ahead of a strong cold front, produced brief wind gusts of 50 to 65 mph during the late night hours.  An unofficial wind sensor in Thornville of northern Perry County measured a wind gust to 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported 4 to 6 inch diameter trees were blown down in scattered locations along County Roads 19, 6 and 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved east across northern Ohio during the afternoon and early evening hours of December 24th.  Gusty winds occurred behind this front.  Peak winds at most locations were between 45 and 50 mph.  However, a few higher gusts were recorded by automated wind sensors.  A few trees and power lines were reported down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An automated wind sensor at the Lorain County Airport measured a 60 mph wind gust at 6:06 pm.  A few trees and large limbs were downed.  A few small power outages were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure passed to the north of Lake Erie during the morning hours of December 21st.  A strong cold front trailing this low swept east across Northeast Ohio.  Cold air moving across Lake Erie behind this front caused lake effect snow showers to develop during the afternoon of the 21st.  Heavy bands of lake effect snow affected the immediate lake shore area.  The heaviest snow fell during the evening hours of the 21st and the afternoon hours of the 22nd.  Strong winds accompanied the snow showers and caused considerable blowing and drifting. Storm total accumulations exceeded a foot in at a couple locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow showers developed during the afternoon of December 21st and continued into the evening hours of the 22nd.  Bands of snow affected the immediate lake shore area with the heaviest snow reported during the late evening hours of the 21st and early morning hours of the 22nd and also on afternoon of the 22nd.  Southwest to west winds gusting to as much as 35 mph accompanied the snow and caused near whiteout conditions at times.  Significant drifting was also reported.  This activity finally diminished during the evening of the 22nd as winds became more southwesterly.  Eight to twelve inches of snow was reported over portions of northern Ashtabula County during this event.  Two snow spotters in the City of Ashtabula reported storm totals of 14.5 and 11.6 inches of snow respectively.  Travel was severely hampered during this event and many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked through central Ohio on the 19th. The low pressure remained just south of the region, but warmer air aloft moved over the area allowing for mainly freezing rain across the region. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation began during the overnight hours of the 19th, and continued through the late morning hours. Precipitation initially was a mixture of snow and sleet across the area, then quickly transitioned over to freezing rain. Freezing rain continued through the late morning hours, and even transitioned over to rain across the southern half of the county before ending. Around a quarter inch of ice was reported across the area. A trained spotter just southwest of Bowling Green reported a quarter inch of ice. In Perrysburg, a dead tree stressed by ice fell onto a house causing minor damage. Overall, the ice impact was fairly minimal as temperatures were around freezing and even rose above freezing before precipitation ended. There were some accidents across the area and some power lines down, but overall damage throughout the area was relatively minor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved northeast into southwest Ohio during the overnight hours of the 1st, then over Lake Erie during the morning hours. Warm air moved over the area intially causing a mix of wintry  precipitation in some locations. However, this wintry mix of precipitation was short lived. Colder air moved in quickly changing the precipitation back to snow during the morning hours of the 1st. The snow eventually tapered off on the morning of the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the early morning hours of the 1st, and continued through the entire day. By the evening of the 1st, snowfall amounts varied widely from 9.0 inches in Solon to only 1.2 inches in Lakewood. Many locations across the area received between 2 and 5 inches on average. At Cleveland Hopkins Airport, snowfall was moderate during the evening hours when visibilities dropped down to a half mile at times. Also, winds gusted between 30 and 35 mph during the afternoon, and there was even a report of thundersnow from a snow spotter in Euclid during the evening hours.  ||The snow continued through the evening and didn't taper off until the early morning hours of the 2nd. By the morning, snowfall reports showed that as much as 10.0 inches fell across the area. Twenty four hour reports ending on the morning of the 2nd indicate that 12.9 inches of snow fell in Solon, 12.0 inches in Lyndhurst, 11.8 inches in Broadview Heights, and 7.9 inches fell at Cleveland Hopkins Airport. Many accidents were reported during the snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved northeast into southwest Ohio during the overnight hours of the 1st, then over Lake Erie during the morning hours. Warm air moved over the area intially causing a mix of wintry  precipitation in some locations. However, this wintry mix of precipitation was short lived. Colder air moved in quickly changing the precipitation back to snow during the morning hours of the 1st. The snow eventually tapered off on the morning of the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the morning hours of the 1st, and continued through the entire day. By evening, as much as 6.0 inches of snow fell in Twinsburg, and 4.5 inches in Richfield. The snow continued during the overnight hours into the 2nd, and tapered off by around daybreak on the 2nd. Highway departments across northern portions of the county reported widespread amounts of at least six inches by around midnight on the 2nd. Twenty four hour totals from the morning of the 1st until the morning of the 2nd include 9.5 inches in Twinsburg and Richfield. Snowfall amounts across the southern half of the county were less, with amounts ranging between 1 and 3 inches. Many accidents were reported with this event, especially across northern portions of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the morning hours of the 1st, and continued through the entire day. Snow during the afternoon hours was moderate at times, and by evening a snow spotter in Hiram already reported 9.5 inches. Law enforcement in cities and towns across northern portions of the county reported between 4 and 7 inches during the late evening hours of the 1st. The snow continued overnight and tapered off around daybreak on the 2nd. A twenty four hour total ending on the morning of the 2nd in Hiram was 12.3 inches. Many other communities across the northern half of the county measured at least 8 inches for this event. Many accidents were reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms along a strong cold front produced wind damage across southern and western Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near Lewisburg and produced scattered damage along a two mile path.  A barn sustained damage along Shields Road.  A house lost part of its roof near the Interstate 70-State Route 503 interchange just south of Lewisburg.  There was extensive tree damage in vicinity of the house, with some of the tree debris dropped into a creek bed behind the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure track north along a cold front from the lower Mississippi River Valley during the day on the 29th and through the central Great Lakes region on the 30th. This area of low pressure intensified as it tracked north through the central Great Lakes from the afternoon on the 29th through the 30th as this low pressure moved into Canada. The cold front associated with this low pressure moved through the region during the early overnight hours into the 30th. Severe thunderstorms developed ahead of the cold front during the late evening on the 29th, but windy conditions continued through the overnight hours as the low pressure intensifed. Winds finally subsided during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A train derailed on a bridge over Sandusky Bay between Erie and Ottawa Counties. Several container cars fell off the bridge and landed in the waters below.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal passage produced strong winds and significant gusts through much of the Ohio valley late in the evening of the 29th and early in the morning of the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured gust of 62 mph occurred in Newark at 12:59 am on the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused flash flooding in Adams County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Eddies Apartment complex in West Union was evacuated due to rising water.  One vehicle was stranded in the high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal passage produced strong winds and significant gusts through much of the Ohio valley late in the evening of the 29th and early in the morning of the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured gust of 58 mph occurred in Wilmington at 11:57 pm on the 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal passage produced strong winds and significant gusts through much of the Ohio valley late in the evening of the 29th and early in the morning of the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured gust of 61 mph occurred at 1:16 am on the 30th at Rickenbacker Field. The ASOS at Port Columbus and at OSU both gusted to 54 mph around the same time.

The strong pressure gradient along and behind the front produced wind gusts near 60 MPH in many locations across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, eastern Ohio, and Garrett county Maryland. This produced numerous reports of trees and power lines down across the region.

Snow spread north into southeast Ohio during Monday evening, the 11th.  The snow accumulated 1 to 3 inches before changing to some freezing rain toward dawn on the 12th, then eventually to spotty light rain later on the 12th.  A quarter inch of ice from freezing rain accumulated in Morgan County with less elsewhere.

Snow overspread the Ohio valley on Thursday night, January 31st, 2008. Overnight, a significant amount of warm air was pulled into the region, changing the precipitation type to freezing rain. By 6 am on the morning of February 1st, enough warm air had been pulled north by the system to change to plain rain for most of the forecast area with above freezing surface temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulated to a quarter inch in Saint Marys.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved northeast across Ohio on February 12th and crossed eastern Lake Erie early on the 13th.  Precipitation associated with this storm spread into northern Ohio during the late evening hours of February 11th.  This precipitation started as snow but eventually transitioned to freezing rain from southwest to northeast during the afternoon hours of the 12th.  The greatest snow amounts occurred across inland sections of Northeast Ohio where the transition to freezing rain occurred the latest. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossed the northern Ohio Valley during the early morning hours of Friday the 19th. Cold air was in place when rain began ahead of the approaching low. Significant icing occurred through about daybreak, when warmer air got pulled north of the low's easterly track.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through the central Great Lakes on the 8th, and continued to slowly track into Ontario on the 9th. Southerly winds ahead of this low pressure allowed for many locations in northern Ohio to report record high temperatures in the 60s during the day on the 8th. The low pressure intensified rapidly as it moved over the Great Lakes during this time. A strong cold front extended from this low pressure, and tracked across the area during the mornning hours of the 9th. A line of showers and thunderstorms developed just ahead of the cold front causing some thunderstorm wind gust damage. Behind the cold front, the strong low pressure allowed for windy conditions to continue through the morning hours of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three power poles were reported down, and wires were in the road. Also, a large limb was reported down.

Straight line winds blew apart a door to a barn. Boards from the barn pierced through the side of the house in two locations putting holes in the wall. A nearby tree only had branches broken from it.

The severe winds were strong enough to cause the shingles to break through the vinyl siding and plywood exterior of the neighboring house. Shingles were actually found in the attic of the neighboring home. Law enforcement reports from that evening also indicate that several trees were down across the City of Norwalk.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quarter inch of ice was reported in Wapakoneta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed during the late evening ahead of a strong cold front. Persistent heavy rain caused flooding, especially across west central Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three homes along US Routes 62 and 68 north of Ripley had their roofs either removed or damaged.  Additional structural damage occurred to a motel, and apartment building and church near Aberdeen.  A number of parked vehicles had windows damaged.  Numerous trees were also uprooted or downed in the area.  Damage was determined to be caused by straight-line winds estimated at 80-90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed during the late evening ahead of a strong cold front. Persistent heavy rain caused flooding, especially across west central Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large portions of the cities of St. Marys and Wapakoneta were flooded.  A trailer park along the Auglaize River just west of Wapakoneta was evacuated and some rescues took place.  Numerous roads were flooded and closed between St. Marys and Wapakoneta.  Many homes sustained basement flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast from the Tennessee Valley over the Ohio River by the morning hours of the 26th. Snow spread across the region during the late evening hours of the 25th and through the overnight hours into the 26th. Low pressure tracked towards the east coast by the evening of the 26th. Snow gradually tapered off across the area by the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th across western areas, but continued through the midday hours of the 27th across northeastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the afternoon hours of the 25th but tapered off to scattered flurries intially during the evening hours of the 25th. Snow picked up again overnight into the 26th and continued through the day. By the evening of the 26th, most locations picked up between 5.0 and 8.0 inches. Snow continued overnight and was heavy at times into the 27th. Visibilities frequently dropped below 2 miles during the afternoon and overnight hours of the 26th into the 27th. By the morning of the 27th, 15.0 inches was reported in Chardon, 12.0 inches in Thompson, and 8.0 inches in Russel Township. Several accidents were reported across the area during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell causing several feet of water to cover roadways throughout the area. State Route 53 west of Upper Sandusky was reported as being washed out during the early morning hours of the 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked to the Ohio Valley as cold air at the surface was already entrenched. This resulted in snow and significant ice accumulations over much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter located 2 miles west of Urbana measured a quarter inch of ice and an inch and a half of snow. The county garage in Urbana reported a trace of glaze with an inch and a half of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county garage in Troy recorded a quarter inch of ice with an inch of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county garage in Clayton recorded a quarter inch of ice with 2.8 inches of snow. A spotter in Miamisburg measured a tenth of an inch of ice with two inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Fairborn measured 3 inches of snow and a quarter inch of ice. A spotter in Spring Valley had 2.8 inches of snow and a tenth of an inch of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain spread into southeast Ohio near dawn on Tuesday, the 18th.  A strong east to west warm front had set up across northern Kentucky.  By late afternoon, rain amounts of 0.5 to 1.35 had already fallen, with the heaviest being over northern Jackson, Vinton, and Athens Counties.  A lull occurred Tuesday evening, as the warm front and its rain shifted north.  However, as the low pressure lifted northeast through the Ohio Valley, another 1 to 2 inches of rain fell on the 19th.  Some preliminary rain totals over about a 36 hour period included, 3.5 inches at McArthur, 3.0 inches at New Lexington, 2.7 inches at Jackson, 2.6 inches at Salem Center, 2.3 inches at McConnelsville, 2.2 inches at Beverly, 2.1 inches at Athens, and 1.7 inches at Nelsonville. Storm totals were even higher further southwest, down the Ohio River Valley, where the moisture inflow was greater.  ||Luckily, southeast Ohio avoided a major flood event. Small stream flooding was widespread across Perry, Athens, Morgan, Vinton, Jackson, and Meigs Counties.  A few streams, such as Duck Creek, also flooded in Washington County.  Numerous roads were closed by high water, but no dwellings were damaged by flood waters.  Several school districts closed schools on the 19th.  County maintenance departments were kept busy clearing debris off of culverts and roads.||Later, the larger streams and rivers also saw minor flooding on the 19th into the 20th.  The Muskingum River creasted just over 12 feet at McConnelsville during the predawn hours of the 20th.  Flood stage there is 11 feet.  The Hocking River at Athens crested at 21.4 feet during the late evening of the 20th.  Flood stage at Athens is 20 feet.  The Shade River near Chester crested at 20 feet during the evening of the 20th.  Bankfull is 17 feet near Chester.

Low pressure moved across central Indiana during the evening hours of the 25th into much of the 26th with widespread snow across northwest Ohio. Most locations seen from four to 7 inches with some sleet mixed in along and south of US 30. A man was killed near Antwerp in Paulding County when his vehicle collided with a semi in the opposing lane on US 24. The driver of the semi was not injured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to seven inches of snow fell across the county.

Winds were gusty across the area causing some blowing and drifting of snow, especially during the morning and early afternoon hours. The snow gradually tapered off during the late afternoon and through the evening hours of the 26th. Many accidents were reported across the area.

There were some locations, mainly in far southern areas of the county, that experienced some brief periods of sleet and freezing rain during the day on the 26th. The snow tapered off through the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th. Total snow accumulations ranged between 7.0 and 9.0 inches throughout the county. There were a few accidents reported during the storm.

Total snow accumulations across the area ranged between 6.0 and 9.0 inches. Gusty winds caused some blowing and drifting of snow and many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm developed in the Southern Plains and tracked into the area with a swath of mainly snow for northern Indiana. The precipitation did start as a period of sleet and even freezing rain. Most areas north and west of a Payne to Napoleon line received 6 to 8 inches of snow along with blowing and drifting snow. South of this line, lesser amounts of snow and sleet were observed, but upwards of two tenths to locally as high as a quarter inch of ice was observed. Many schools closed for the day, giving students a long weekend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow and sleet, as well as up to two tenths of an inch of ice, caused difficult travel conditions.

Snow spread across the region during the late evening hours of the 25th and through the overnight hours into the 26th. Low pressure tracked towards the east coast by the evening of the 26th. Snow gradually tapered off across the area by the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th across western areas, but continued through the midday hours of the 27th across northeastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the late evening hours of the 25th, and continued through the day on the 26th. By early afternoon of the 26th, highway departments in the county measured at least 6.0 inches of snow. Snow continued through the evening and ended during the early morning hours of the 27th. By the end of the event, between 6.0 and 10.0 inches of snow was reported throughout the area. Considering blowing and drifting of snow occurred during the midday and afternoon hours of the 26th. At the Findley Airport winds frequently gusted to around 25 mph and visibilities dropped below one mile. During the late morning hours the visibility dropped below a half mile at the airport. Many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast from the Tennessee Valley over the Ohio River by the morning hours of the 26th. Snow spread across the region during the late evening hours of the 25th and through the overnight hours into the 26th. Low pressure tracked towards the east coast by the evening of the 26th. Snow gradually tapered off across the area by the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th across western areas, but continued through the midday hours of the 27th across northeastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the late evening hours of the 25th and continued through the day on the 26th. By midday on the 26th, highway departments and public reports indicate that between 6.0 and 8.0 inches of snow already accumulated across the area. Snow continued through the afternoon and tapered off overnight into the 27th. By the end of the event between 7.0 and 10.0 inches of snow fell across the area. Gusty winds allowed for some blowing and drifting of snow throughout the county and numerous accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second major storm in less than a week affected southeast Ohio.  A low pressure system lifted north from Georgia on Friday the 7th, through the Smoky Mountains that night, then into the mountainous counties of West Virginia by dawn on the 8th.||Southeast Ohio was in the transition zone. Heavier snow fell to the west, while rain and lighter snow occurred to the east.||There were several phases to the storm.  Wet snow, rain, and freezing rain overspread southeast Ohio during the day on the 7th from the south.  A prolonged lull in the precipitation occurred that evening, with just some spotty light snow and freezing rain.  This lull started to break down after midnight with snow, sleet, and freezing rain redeveloping. Another lull occurred during late morning hours.  The enhanced snow that had been falling over western portions of Ohio finally started to swing east during the early afternoon on the 8th, increasing the snow showers for the final phase.  The snow showers then decreased by nightfall that Saturday evening, the 8th.  ||Five to 10 inches of snow were common across Jackson, Vinton, Perry, and Morgan Counties, plus western portions of Athens and Meigs Counties.  Further east, in Washington County and eastern portions of Meigs and Athens Counties, only 2 to 4 inches of snow fell over a quarter inch of ice from freezing rain.

Low pressure moved northeast from the Tennessee Valley over the Ohio River by the morning hours of the 26th. Snow spread across the region during the late evening hours of the 25th and through the overnight hours into the 26th. Low pressure tracked towards the east coast by the evening of the 26th. Snow gradually tapered off across the area by the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th across western areas, but continued through the midday hours of the 27th across northeastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began late on the 25th and continued through much of the day on the 26th. The heaviest snow fell during the overnight and morning hours of the 26th. Snow tapered off during the afternoon and finally came to an end during the evening. By midday on the 26th, 6.5 inches of snow was reported at Bowling Green. Highway departments in the county measured between 6.0 and 8.0 inches of snow from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rains of around a half inch fell in less than 3 hours just before dawn on Friday, the 4th.  This was out ahead of an approaching cold front.  Prior to these heavier showers, widespread rain fell on Thursday, the 3rd, from a warm front.  Total rainfall in less than 36 hours was 1.5 to 2.25 inches across Lawrence and Gallia Counties.||Minor small stream flooding occurred on Friday morning.  A few of the specific streams included Indian Guyan Creek in Lawrence County.  It flooded Route 218 near Platform.  In Greenfield Township of Gallia County, Dirtyface Creek flooded.||No structural damage to dwelling was reported.
Low pressure tracked to the Ohio Valley as cold air at the surface was already entrenched. This resulted in snow and significant ice accumulations over much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager in Portsmouth measured an inch of snow and a half inch of ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast from the Tennessee Valley over the Ohio River by the morning hours of the 26th. Snow spread across the region during the late evening hours of the 25th and through the overnight hours into the 26th. Low pressure tracked towards the east coast by the evening of the 26th. Snow gradually tapered off across the area by the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th across western areas, but continued through the midday hours of the 27th across northeastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began overnight late on the 25th, and continued through the overnight hours. By morning snowfall amounts ranged between 3.0 and 5.5 inches across the county. By midday on the 26th, many locations picked up between 6.0 and 8.0 inches. At Toledo Express Airport 6.7 inches of snow fell, Sylvania measured 7.0 inches, and Maumee reported 6.0 inches of snow. Highway departments in the county also measured at least 6.0 inches of snow for this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast from the Tennessee Valley over the Ohio River by the morning hours of the 26th. Snow spread across the region during the late evening hours of the 25th and through the overnight hours into the 26th. Low pressure tracked towards the east coast by the evening of the 26th. Snow gradually tapered off across the area by the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th across western areas, but continued through the midday hours of the 27th across northeastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began late on the 25th and continued through the 26th. The snow was light during the overnight hours into the 26th, but picked up from late morning and through the afternoon. By evening, around 7.0 inches of snow fell across the area. Visibilities during the late morning and afternoon dropped to less than three miles. At times visibilities dropped to a mile or less. Winds gusted to around 30 mph causing some blowing and drifting of snow. Some locations, especially across the southern half of the county mixed with sleet and freezing rain during the late morning and afternoon. Snow continued overnight and finally tapered off to flurries by midday on the 27th. Snowfall totals ranged from 7.0 to 10.0 inches across the area with heaviest amounts across the northern half of the county. Many accidents were reported during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast from the Tennessee Valley over the Ohio River by the morning hours of the 26th. Snow spread across the region during the late evening hours of the 25th and through the overnight hours into the 26th. Low pressure tracked towards the east coast by the evening of the 26th. Snow gradually tapered off across the area by the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th across western areas, but continued through the midday hours of the 27th across northeastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began late on the 25th, and continued through the 26th. By the morning of the 26th, between 3.0 and 5.0 inches of snow fell across the area. Snow continued through the day and gusty winds around 30 mph caused some blowing and drifting snow. Visibilities were also reduced during the daytime hours to as low as one mile at times. The snow continued overnight and gradually tapered off during the morning and midday hours of the 27th. By the end of the event snowfall totals ranged between 7.0 and 9.0 inches with around 8.0 inches in Sandusky and Vermilion. Many accidents were reported from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast from the Tennessee Valley over the Ohio River by the morning hours of the 26th. Snow spread across the region during the late evening hours of the 25th and through the overnight hours into the 26th. Low pressure tracked towards the east coast by the evening of the 26th. Snow gradually tapered off across the area by the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th across western areas, but continued through the midday hours of the 27th across northeastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began around midnight on the 26th and continued through the day. Snow finally tapered off to flurries and snow showers around midday on the 27th. On the morning of the 26th, spotters reported around 3.0 inches. As the day progressed, visibilities were frequently two miles or less, and winds gusts between 25 and 30 mph were common during the afternoon and evening hours. By the morning of the 27th, snowfall amounts ranged between 8.0 and 11.0 inches with 10.5 inches in North Ridgeville and 10.3 inches in Wellington. Numerous accidents were reported from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast from the Tennessee Valley over the Ohio River by the morning hours of the 26th. Snow spread across the region during the late evening hours of the 25th and through the overnight hours into the 26th. Low pressure tracked towards the east coast by the evening of the 26th. Snow gradually tapered off across the area by the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th across western areas, but continued through the midday hours of the 27th across northeastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began late in the day on the 25th and continued through the day on the 26th. The tapered off gradually during the early morning hours of the 27th and ended in the afternoon. Snow spotters reported between 3.0 and 5.0 inches fell by the morning of the 26th. Snow continued through the day, and visibilities dropped to a mile or less at times throughout the day. Winds gusted between 35 and 40 mph at times during the afternoon and evening hours causing blowing and drifting snow. By the morning of the 27th, 9.7 inches of snow fell in New London, and around 8.0 inches fell in Norwalk. Highway departments in the area reported between 7.0 and 10.0 inches of snow for this event. Many accidents were reported across the area during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm developed in the Southern Plains and tracked into the area with a swath of mainly snow for northern Indiana. The precipitation did start as a period of sleet and even freezing rain. Most areas north and west of a Payne to Napoleon line received 6 to 8 inches of snow along with blowing and drifting snow. South of this line, lesser amounts of snow and sleet were observed, but upwards of two tenths to locally as high as a quarter inch of ice was observed. Many schools closed for the day, giving students a long weekend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of snow and sleet, caused difficult travel conditions.

Low pressure moved northeast from the Tennessee Valley over the Ohio River by the morning hours of the 26th. Snow spread across the region during the late evening hours of the 25th and through the overnight hours into the 26th. Low pressure tracked towards the east coast by the evening of the 26th. Snow gradually tapered off across the area by the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th across western areas, but continued through the midday hours of the 27th across northeastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began late on the 25th across the area and continued through the day on the 26th. By the late morning hours of the 26th, spotters reported between 1.0 and 3.0 inches of snow. Snow continued through the afternoon and evening hours. Spotters reported in some locations that snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain at times. The snow gradually tapered off overnight into the 27th but didn't come to an end until around midday of the 27th. By the end of the event, snowfall reports ranged between 6.0 and 9.0 inches. Bucyrus picked up around 7.5 inches from this event. Southern areas of the county that mixed with sleet and freezing rain measured only between 3.0 and 6.0 inches of snow. Many accidents were reported from the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved northeast from the Tennessee Valley over the Ohio River by the morning hours of the 26th. Snow spread across the region during the late evening hours of the 25th and through the overnight hours into the 26th. Low pressure tracked towards the east coast by the evening of the 26th. Snow gradually tapered off across the area by the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th across western areas, but continued through the midday hours of the 27th across northeastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began across the area late on the 25th and continued for the next day and a half. Snow was heaviest across the area on the 26th. Only a few inches of snow fell by the morning hours of the 26th, but snow picked up quickly late in the morning and through the afternoon. Visibilities during the late morning and early afternoon hours dropped down to a mile or less. By evening between 6.0 and 8.0 inches to accumulate across the area. Winds gusted to around 30 mph at times causing some blowing and drifting of snow. The snow persisted across the area through midday of the 27th, and by the end of the event between 7.0 and 10.0 inches of snow fell. Numerous accidents were reported during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain spread into southeast Ohio near dawn on Tuesday, the 18th.  A strong east to west warm front had set up across northern Kentucky.  By late afternoon, rain amounts of 0.5 to 1.35 had already fallen, with the heaviest being over northern Jackson, Vinton, and Athens Counties.  A lull occurred Tuesday evening, as the warm front and its rain shifted north.  However, as the low pressure lifted northeast through the Ohio Valley, another 1 to 2 inches of rain fell on the 19th.  Some preliminary rain totals over about a 36 hour period included, 3.5 inches at McArthur, 3.0 inches at New Lexington, 2.7 inches at Jackson, 2.6 inches at Salem Center, 2.3 inches at McConnelsville, 2.2 inches at Beverly, 2.1 inches at Athens, and 1.7 inches at Nelsonville. Storm totals were even higher further southwest, down the Ohio River Valley, where the moisture inflow was greater.  ||Luckily, southeast Ohio avoided a major flood event. Small stream flooding was widespread across Perry, Athens, Morgan, Vinton, Jackson, and Meigs Counties.  A few streams, such as Duck Creek, also flooded in Washington County.  Numerous roads were closed by high water, but no dwellings were damaged by flood waters.  Several school districts closed schools on the 19th.  County maintenance departments were kept busy clearing debris off of culverts and roads.||Later, the larger streams and rivers also saw minor flooding on the 19th into the 20th.  The Muskingum River creasted just over 12 feet at McConnelsville during the predawn hours of the 20th.  Flood stage there is 11 feet.  The Hocking River at Athens crested at 21.4 feet during the late evening of the 20th.  Flood stage at Athens is 20 feet.  The Shade River near Chester crested at 20 feet during the evening of the 20th.  Bankfull is 17 feet near Chester.

Low pressure moved northeast from the Tennessee Valley over the Ohio River by the morning hours of the 26th. Snow spread across the region during the late evening hours of the 25th and through the overnight hours into the 26th. Low pressure tracked towards the east coast by the evening of the 26th. Snow gradually tapered off across the area by the afternoon and evening hours of the 26th across western areas, but continued through the midday hours of the 27th across northeastern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began around midnight on the 26th and continued through the remainder of the day. Visibilities at times were less than a mile during the heavier periods of snow. Snow finally tapered off during  the morning hours of the 27th. By the end of the event, many locations picked up between 6.0 and 9.0 inches with 7.5 inches measured in Pierpont. Many accidents were reported during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved across central Indiana during the evening hours of the 25th into much of the 26th with widespread snow across northwest Ohio. Most locations seen from four to 7 inches with some sleet mixed in along and south of US 30. A man was killed near Antwerp in Paulding County when his vehicle collided with a semi in the opposing lane on US 24. The driver of the semi was not injured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to seven inches of snow fell across the county along with some sleet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved across central Indiana during the evening hours of the 25th into much of the 26th with widespread snow across northwest Ohio. Most locations seen from four to 7 inches with some sleet mixed in along and south of US 30. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to seven inches of snow fell across the county. Weather was at least partially to blame for a 37 year old man being killed when the vehicle he was driving went into the opposing lane and was struck by a semi on US 24 near Carryall Township Road 51. The driver of the semi was not injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snowpack of one to three inches rapidly melted as warm air arrive in the region. This snowmelt, combined with a partially frozen, very moist ground and rainfall from two to locally over 3 inches, resulted in an increase in low land and river flooding running south of a Paulding to Ottawa line. At the onset, some flash flooding occurred in areas experiencing rainfall rates of one-half to one inch per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported three areas of water covered roadway on US 24 and Ohio 424 along the Maumee River. Closure of a bridge crossing over Turkey Foot Creek, southeast of Napoleon prompted the closure of County Road P.

An area of low pressure moved northeast across Ohio on February 12th and crossed eastern Lake Erie early on the 13th.  Precipitation associated with this storm spread into northern Ohio during the late evening hours of February 11th.  This precipitation started as snow but eventually transitioned to freezing rain from southwest to northeast during the afternoon hours of the 12th.  The greatest snow amounts occurred across inland sections of Northeast Ohio where the transition to freezing rain occurred the latest.  The precitipation quickly ended from west to east during the evening of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow spread into Medina County around midnight on February 12th and continued through the early afternoon hours of the 12th.  At that time, the precipitation transitioned to freezing rain.  The freezing rain ended during the evening hours of the 12th.  Two to four inches of snow was reported across the county along with up to a quarter inch of ice accumulation.  Many accidents and a few power outages were reported.

An area of low pressure moved northeast across Ohio on February 12th and crossed eastern Lake Erie early on the 13th.  Precipitation associated with this storm spread into northern Ohio during the late evening hours of February 11th.  This precipitation started as snow but eventually transitioned to freezing rain from southwest to northeast during the afternoon hours of the 12th.  The greatest snow amounts occurred across inland sections of Northeast Ohio where the transition to freezing rain occurred the latest.  The precitipation quickly ended from west to east during the evening of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow spread into Summit County during the late evening hours of February 11th and continued through the early afternoon hours of the 12th.  At that time, the precipitation transitioned to freezing rain.  The freezing rain briefly changed to rain before ending during the evening hours of the 12th.  Four to six inches of snow was reported across the county along with up to a quarter inch of ice accumulation.  Many accidents and a few power outages were reported.  Officially, 5.5 inches of snow and 0.16 inches of freezing rain was recorded at the Akron-Canton Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain spread into southeast Ohio near dawn on Tuesday, the 18th.  A strong east to west warm front had set up across northern Kentucky.  By late afternoon, rain amounts of 0.5 to 1.35 had already fallen, with the heaviest being over northern Jackson, Vinton, and Athens Counties.  A lull occurred Tuesday evening, as the warm front and its rain shifted north.  However, as the low pressure lifted northeast through the Ohio Valley, another 1 to 2 inches of rain fell on the 19th.  Some preliminary rain totals over about a 36 hour period included, 3.5 inches at McArthur, 3.0 inches at New Lexington, 2.7 inches at Jackson, 2.6 inches at Salem Center, 2.3 inches at McConnelsville, 2.2 inches at Beverly, 2.1 inches at Athens, and 1.7 inches at Nelsonville. Storm totals were even higher further southwest, down the Ohio River Valley, where the moisture inflow was greater.  ||Luckily, southeast Ohio avoided a major flood event. Small stream flooding was widespread across Perry, Athens, Morgan, Vinton, Jackson, and Meigs Counties.  A few streams, such as Duck Creek, also flooded in Washington County.  Numerous roads were closed by high water, but no dwellings were damaged by flood waters.  Several school districts closed schools on the 19th.  County maintenance departments were kept busy clearing debris off of culverts and roads.||Later, the larger streams and rivers also saw minor flooding on the 19th into the 20th.  The Muskingum River creasted just over 12 feet at McConnelsville during the predawn hours of the 20th.  Flood stage there is 11 feet.  The Hocking River at Athens crested at 21.4 feet during the late evening of the 20th.  Flood stage at Athens is 20 feet.  The Shade River near Chester crested at 20 feet during the evening of the 20th.  Bankfull is 17 feet near Chester.

A snowpack of one to three inches rapidly melted as warm air arrive in the region. This snowmelt, combined with a partially frozen, very moist ground and rainfall from two to locally over 3 inches, resulted in an increase in low land and river flooding running south of a Paulding to Ottawa line. At the onset, some flash flooding occurred in areas experiencing rainfall rates of one-half to one inch per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported that portions of State Route 424 in Defiance county had water on it.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man drowned along a swollen Racoon Creek and a low spot along Route 325 near the village of Vinton.  Otherwise, county officials reported no flooding.  ||According to a state report, the driver drove partially into water covering the road, but then backed out. This was at night. The driver then pulled into a nearby driveway and asked the property owner if he thought he could make it through the water.  The property owner told the driver that if he could see the edge lines, he should be okay.  The driver then proceeded to drive into the water, where his pickup truck was caught by the current and swept downstream.  The driver was able to get out of his truck.  The property owner ran over to try to help, offering a long stick to the driver, telling him to swim toward it.  The driver was unable to make it.  His body was recovered a few hours later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure track north along a cold front from the lower Mississippi River Valley during the day on the 29th and through the central Great Lakes region on the 30th. This area of low pressure intensified as it tracked north through the central Great Lakes from the afternoon on the 29th through the 30th as this low pressure moved into Canada. The cold front associated with this low pressure moved through the region during the early overnight hours into the 30th. Severe thunderstorms developed ahead of the cold front during the late evening on the 29th, but windy conditions continued through the overnight hours as the low pressure intensifed. Winds finally subsided during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large limbs were reported down two miles northeast of Bucyrus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic low passed over Ohio on January 1st. Snowfall started in the wrap around moisture with this system, and by daybreak of the 2nd  there was significant blowing and drifting that made the snow difficult to measure.| |During the daytime hours of January 1st, a lake effect snow band set up through Richmond and southern Dayton to Wilmington. Scattered snow showers were found throughout the region through the afternoon and evening. During the overnight, a heavy band of snow produced 2 to 3 inches of snow in a very short period of time. This caused a heavier snowfall of 4 to 6 inches in a band between Dayton, Xenia, Waynesville, and Wilmington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow fell in Springfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through the central Great Lakes on the 8th, and continued to slowly track into Ontario on the 9th. Southerly winds ahead of this low pressure allowed for many locations in northern Ohio to report record high temperatures in the 60s during the day on the 8th. The low pressure intensified rapidly as it moved over the Great Lakes during this time. A strong cold front extended from this low pressure, and tracked across the area during the mornning hours of the 9th. A line of showers and thunderstorms developed just ahead of the cold front causing some thunderstorm wind gust damage. Behind the cold front, the strong low pressure allowed for windy conditions to continue through the morning hours of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many trees were reported down countywide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow overspread the Ohio valley on Thursday night, January 31st, 2008. Overnight, a significant amount of warm air was pulled into the region, changing the precipitation type to freezing rain. By 6 am on the morning of February 1st, enough warm air had been pulled north by the system to change to plain rain for most of the forecast area with above freezing surface temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulated to a quarter inch in Rushsylvania.

Travel was extremely hazardous with disruption to schools and some businesses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracked from Arkansas into central Ohio, bringing a swath of precipitation to all of northwestern Ohio. The heaviest snow fell across far northwestern Ohio across Williams County, where around six inches fell. South of Williams County, amounts were generally in the one to four inch range as sleet and some freezing rain mixed in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported between one quarter and one half inch of sleet, along with one to three inches of snow and some freezing rain. This caused slippery travel conditions across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure tracked from the southern Appalachians to western Virginia by Saturday March 8th. A deepening upper level trough worked into the Ohio Valley overnight, combining with the surface low and strengthening the entire system. The surface pressure gradient tightened in response to the upper low, and gusts of 30 to 40 mph brought blizzard-like conditions to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thirteen inches of snow was measured in Jacksontown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front worked across the area during the afternoon and evening hours of the 11th. Strong wind fields were in place along with modest instability to support narrow lines of thunderstorms. A few of these storms became strong and locally severe across northwestern Ohio. Spotters reported pea size hail with many of the storms with a few portions of the line exhibiting some bowing structures as stronger winds attempted to mix down. Some wind damage along with a funnel cloud, was reported in the Fort Jennings area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five telephone poles were damaged from strong winds, with two being snapped off in the Fort Jennings and Ottawaville area. A wood barn was blown down by the strong winds, with two nearby trees being uprooted. Damage is estimated at $15,000 for repair and replacement of the poles and $50,000 for the replacement of the barn.
